records.ety: [OF. aplier, F. appliquer, fr. L. applicare to join, fix, or attach to; ad + plicare to fold, to twist together. See Applicant, Ply.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Applied (<?/); p. pr. & vb. n. Applying.]

defs: To lay or place; to put or adjust (one thing to another); -- with to; as, to apply the hand to the breast; to apply medicaments to a diseased part of the body.
quotes: He said, and the sword his throat applied.

defs: To put to use; to use or employ for a particular purpose, or in a particular case; to appropriate; to devote; as, to apply money to the payment of a debt.
To make use of, declare, or pronounce, as suitable, fitting, or relative; as, to apply the testimony to the case; to apply an epithet to a person.
quotes: Yet God at last To Satan, first in sin, his doom applied.

defs: To fix closely; to engage and employ diligently, or with attention; to attach; to incline.

quotes: Sacred vows . . . applied to grisly Pluto.

defs: To betake; to address; to refer; -- used reflexively.
quotes: I applied myself to him for help.

defs: To busy; to keep at work; to ply.
quotes: She was skillful in applying his “humors.”
And he applied each place so fast.

defs: To suit; to agree; to have some connection, agreement, or analogy; as, this argument applies well to the case.
To make request; to have recourse with a view to gain something; to make application. (to); to solicit; as, to apply to a friend for information.

quotes: I heard the sound of an oar applying swiftly through the water.

defs: To apply or address one's self; to give application; to attend closely (to).
